title: The Year-Ahead Profit Plan Most Owner-Led Businesses Never Build
linkedin:
Most businesses closing FY26 right now know what the year delivered. Far fewer will enter July with an explicit profit target for the twelve months ahead.

The annual P&L does exactly what it was designed for: it records what happened. Your bookkeeper and accountant produce it with care for the ATO, the tax return, and the financial statements.

What the P&L cannot do is tell you whether the result was the one you intended. Whether gross margin held at target. Whether an overhead line absorbed more profit than the plan allowed. Whether the revenue mix drifted toward lower-margin work without a decision to go there.

These questions need a budget, but a specific kind. Not a backward extrapolation with a percentage added, but a forward model of what the business intends to produce and the cost structure required to produce it.

The insight most budgets miss is where to start. Not with a total revenue target. With a gross margin target by revenue category, because gross margin is where profitability lives and the metric most directly in your control through pricing, service mix, and cost of delivery decisions.

June is the natural moment for this work. The prior year's data is complete right now. Businesses that build the budget in September are already three months into the year they are planning for.

A budget without monthly variance reporting is a document. With it, the business knows in October when something has moved, while there is still time to respond. That is the difference between managing toward what is still possible, and managing from what already happened.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Do you have an explicit profit target set for FY27 yet?

Not revenue. Profit. And specifically, the gross margin percentage you intend to hold by revenue category, and the cost structure required to support it.

Most businesses close June 30 knowing what the year delivered. Few enter July with a clear forward model of what they are building toward.

The EOFY data sitting in your accounting system right now is the richest financial picture of the year. Using it to build a forward plan before July 1 is more precise than building it in August, after the picture has moved on.

New post on the blog: https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-year-ahead-profit-plan-most-owner-led-businesses-never-build

google:
With the financial year about to close, most owner-led businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria are reviewing twelve months of trading data for the first time. The question most use it to answer is: what happened?

A more useful question is what the year ahead looks like from a profit perspective.

A management budget built from the EOFY data answers that. It starts not with a total revenue target but with a gross margin target by revenue category, because gross margin is where profitability sits and the metric most directly in your control through pricing, service mix, and delivery costs.

The cost structure follows the gross margin target. Each overhead line is reviewed against the expected revenue level for the year ahead. Some costs grow with volume; some stay fixed. Understanding which is which determines how much of each additional revenue dollar flows through to profit, and what happens if revenue comes in below plan for a quarter.

A budget without monthly variance reporting stays a document. With it, the business knows in October when something has moved, and can respond before a full year of drift has accumulated.

The June window for this work is closing. Businesses that build their FY27 budget from the current data are working from the most complete picture available. Businesses that start in September are already three months into the year they are planning for.

If your FY27 profit target is not yet explicit, the new ProfitPulse post walks through what a management budget actually contains and why the timing matters right now.

https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-year-ahead-profit-plan-most-owner-led-businesses-never-build
